chapters.summary: Council staff opens the meeting of the Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises, reminds attendees to silence devices and not approach the dais.
chapters.title: Subcommittee called to order and housekeeping

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Good afternoon, everyone, and welcome to today's New York City Council vote for the Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: At this time, please silence all electronic devices.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Just a reminder, no one may approach the dais at any time.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Thank you.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair, we are ready to begin.

chapters.summary: Chair Farah Louis calls the meeting to order, introduces fellow council members, and announces the subcommittee will vote on three proposals covering LU 72 through 77.
chapters.title: Chair Louis introduces the subcommittee and agenda

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Good afternoon and welcome to a meeting of the Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I am Councilmember Farrah Lewis, Chair of the Subcommittee.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This afternoon, I am joined by Councilmembers Farias, Shulman, Salam, Felder, Encarnacion, Thomas, Henry, Carr, and Jay Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Stand at ease.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis describes the first development site at 801 11th Avenue, seeking a mixed-use residential building with approximately 477 apartments, 125 permanently affordable, and a zoning map amendment from M2-3 to C4-7.
chapters.title: Chair Louis outlines Dewitt Clinton Park North proposal — 801 11th Avenue site

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Today we are scheduled to vote on three proposals.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We will vote on LU 72 through 77.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Regarding the Do It Clinton Park North proposal, which actually consists of two separate development sites, these sites are located on the west side of Manhattan in the northern part of Hell's Kitchen and are in Councilmember Brewer's District.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The first site is located at 801 11th Avenue along 11th Avenue, and there

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: the applicant is seeking to develop a mixed-use residential building that will have approximately 477 apartments, of which approximately 125 will be permanently affordable.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis explains the second site at 629 West 54th Street with approximately 617 apartments, 162 affordable, and the proposed transfer of floor area from Hudson River Park piers in exchange for $229.7 million to the Hudson River Park Trust.
chapters.title: Chair Louis describes 629 West 54th Street site and Hudson River Park floor area transfer

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The second site is located at 629 West 54th Street along 12th Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And the applicant is seeking to develop another mixed-use residential building that will have approximately 617 apartments, of which approximately 162 will be permanently affordable.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The proposed developments would seek a transfer of floor area

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: from the commercial park piers in Hudson River Park.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: in exchange for approximately $229.7 million payment

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: to Hudson River Park Trust that would support improvements in capital maintenance in the park.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Both sites require the same set of app

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: of actions.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis outlines the zoning map amendments, text amendments for mandatory inclusionary housing, special permits for floor area transfer, and recommended modifications including removal of the street wall waiver and reducing building height from 550 to 520 feet.
chapters.title: Chair Louis details zoning actions and recommended modifications for Dewitt Clinton Park North

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The 11th Avenue site is requesting a zoning map amendment to change the existing manufacturing M2-3 zoning district to a commercial

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: C4-district, which has a residential equivalent of a R10 district.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The 12th Avenue site is also requesting a zoning map amendment to change the existing manufacturing M2-4 zoning district to a commercial C4-7 district.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Second, a zoning tax amendment would map a mandatory inclusionary housing area on both sites, as well as an update.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The special Hudson River Park District rules to reflect the new granting and receiving sites for a floor area transfer from Hudson River Park

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: and clarify the affordable, automated-related use provisions for C4-7 districts, as well as applicable bulk rules and the app.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Applicability of the special Clinton district regulations.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The third action, again, applicable to both

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: is a special permit to allow the transfer of a development right from Hudson River Park to the development sites and to provide bulk waivers to facilitate the proposed design of the mixed-use buildings.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We are recommending the following modifications.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The removal of the street wall location waiver granted by CPC for the 11th Avenue site, which will provide a more consistent streetscape along 11th Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: For the 12th Avenue site, the modification is to reduce the building height from the CPC approved 550 feet to a maximum of 520 feet.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Consistent with the intent of having the height of buildings decreased from 11th and 12th Avenue in order to create less of a barrier along the waterfront.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Councilmember Brewer supports these applications as modified.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I now recognize Councilmember Brewer for a statement.

chapters.summary: Brewer explains the two separately owned buildings, the FAR transfer from Hudson River Park Trust generating at least $29 million, and extensive community board engagement with CB4.
chapters.title: Council Member Brewer on Dewitt Clinton Park North — project overview and Hudson River Park Trust payment

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you, Madam Chair.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: These two buildings, as you suggested, are being considered together in the area of 54th Street and 11th Avenue.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They are separately owned.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Most of the discussions were with Mr.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Freeland and not the Chapman organization when we spoke to them.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And they are getting what we call Farah, which is basically height, from Hudson River Park Trust.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's a good project where Hudson River Park Trust, which, you know, every time you look at a...

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Peer that needs assistance like peer 76 it's

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: millions and millions of dollars.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So the least that these buildings will pay Hudson River Park Trust is $29 million, which is around $200 a square foot.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We also think that maybe they should pay more, but that's what it is at the moment, and they'll have to work it out.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: There have been many, many meetings between Community Board 4 and the developers, and I participated in them, both in person at the Community Board and then Zoom discussions with these parties, as well as the Council Land Use staff.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And I certainly want to thank Paul Devlin and Joe Restitio from Board 4, as well as the Council Land Use staff here.

chapters.summary: Brewer describes CB4's sophisticated review, their negative declaration unless milestones were met, and the community's desire for greater height reduction than the 30 feet achieved.
chapters.title: Brewer on CB4's conditional support and height reduction negotiations

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: CB4, which is a very sophisticated board, with all due respect to all other 58 community boards, nobody else has the expertise like Board 4.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The people on it have been doing this for a long time, particularly Joe Vestigia.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They support housing at this site, but just so you know, at the full board meeting that I was at, they issued a negative declaration, unless certain milestones were met, as opposed to a yes, but, which is what they would have liked to,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: because the owners were so reluctant to make any concessions for the community.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The height, community wanted the max height to come down, but the most that we could get was a reduced 30 feet from 550 to 520.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They wanted to bring it down even more because don't understand these buildings are facing the river.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You want to have as much

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: and air on the river as possible for the fish and other aspects of marine life, but we were not able to get it down even further.

chapters.summary: Brewer highlights the city park across the street needing $6 million bathroom and $6 million playground renovations, but the developer only committed $200,000 over several years.
chapters.title: Brewer on DeWitt Clinton Park renovation needs and limited developer contribution

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Another issue, the Whit Clinton Park is right across the street.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This is not the Hudson River Park.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Hudson River Park is on the river.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The Hudson River, the Whit Clinton Park is a city park.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's right across the street from these buildings.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I know it well.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It includes ball fields, leisure areas, playgrounds, and more.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The Parks Department sent us a memo indicating that bathroom renovation is a little over $6 million, and a play area also needs the same renovation at about $6 million.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And understand that the many, many units in these buildings, thousands of units, will of course look over the park, number one, and two, use the park.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But the most we could get out of the owners was $200,000 over a few years, so it comes in depending on the CFO or the temporary CFO, despite the request for more, six plus six.

chapters.summary: Brewer details the fight for additional workforce housing beyond MIH requirements, settling for 1.25% additional permanent affordable units (12-13 units) after extensive negotiation, and the importance of rent stabilization.
chapters.title: Brewer on affordability negotiations and workforce housing

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Then of course there's the issue of affordability.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: By law, as you heard from the chair, 25% to 30%, depending on option one or option two, are mandated to be affordable and to be low income.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's based on law and based on the state.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Community Board 4 has always advocated, unlike other boards, what I call workforce housing, which is basically higher

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: income, teachers, firefighters, police, et cetera.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And we wanted to add another 5%, so it would be 30% total.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We started to compromise at 2.5%, meaning 165% AMI, workforce housing, make it,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: additional to what was there MIH mandated.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But we ended up, after much nickeling and diming, is what I would call it, with one and a quarter percent, maybe 12 to 13 units additional, but they are permanent.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The owners wanted to have

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: just housing that would be affordable for the life of the property tax abatement, which is usually 35 to 40 years.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But having been around for 35 or 40 years, I've seen housing disappear and become market in some of the buildings on the Upper West Side.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So it's less units.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They do mirror and track with the other units in the building.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You cannot build all studios.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: You have to be consistent with the one, two, three studios and one bedrooms with the rest of the buildings.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So it can't be on the ground floor.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It has to be moved around in the other buildings, and it has to track the same size.

chapters.summary: Brewer discusses the developer's dislike of the 485X tax abatement, uncertainty about whether the project will be built, and the existing active union jobs on the site.
chapters.title: Brewer on 485X tax abatement concerns and developer uncertainty

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So the other issue was, I guess Mr.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Freeland, along with everybody else in the development world, hate 485X.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They don't like this new property tax exception that was negotiated and passed in Albany.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: them figure it out what's going to happen.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's not my problem.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But owner has stated that a 485X property tax is not renovated, I shouldn't renovate it, is not enacted in a different way.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: He's not gonna build.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Right now there's a good,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: storefront, meaning there's a ground floor auto repair, auto sales, which is an excellent union jobs, and there's a commercial building on one of the sites.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So it's active currently.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It's not like they're vacant lots.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I don't know what's going to happen if this thing will ever get built.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It all depends on 485X.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They don't like, the developers don't like the union portion of that bill, and they don't like that you have to be 100 units, and then above that, you've got to follow 485X labor law.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I think a lot of folks are building at 99 units, but not here.

chapters.summary: Brewer expresses frustration with HPD's reluctance to subsidize affordable housing in high-income Manhattan neighborhoods, calls on 32BJ to advocate for more workforce housing, and demands rent stabilization for the negotiated units.
chapters.title: Brewer criticizes HPD and calls for more affordable housing in high-income areas

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I support, because we've been working with the community board to come up with this, but I do want to say to the city of New York, to HPD, having been borough president for eight years, it is so darn hard

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: to get any kind of affordable housing in these high-income areas.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And it makes me crazy.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The schools are excellent.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The transportation is excellent.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: The parks are excellent.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: It doesn't seem right that every time I ask for support, subsidy if needed,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: that we are told is cheaper land in the Bronx.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We're going to the Bronx, Gail.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We're not gonna stay in Manhattan.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And that's what's happening here.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I mean, if HPD or others

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I just wanted to say, if the developer's not gonna do this, then we should find other ways to subsidize this apartment building.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: These should have more affordable housing than what we're able to negotiate, number one.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Number two, I'm a big supporter of all of the unions, but when I call 32BJ, and to the credit of the developer,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They're working in terms of long-term 32BJ jobs, as they should.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But 32BJ should say, we want 5% affordable.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: We want the extra workforce housing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They don't.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: They say they go along.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: That's not right.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Every unit in the city should be stating we need more workforce housing.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so I'm here also to say I think HPD is going to work with us to make sure that once the abatement is over,

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: if a family moves out, that market, that union, those few 12, 13, 14 units, they need to stay rate stabilization.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: I have to be very, very clear on that.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So it's written into law.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And they should be rent stabilized.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Not equivalent rent stabilized, but actual rent stabilized.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: So I'm here to be supportive, but I'm also here after, what, 25 years, 200 and some ULURPs I've done.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: This is really nickeling and diming, and it's not building affordable housing the way that we should be doing it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And so I hope that this is somewhat of a lesson.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: And then I think it's sort of putting everybody on notice that good

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Intention is not good enough.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: What is the only way that we build affordable housing is to actually build it.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: If you want to put in a subsidy, fine.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: But nickeling and diming is not the way to go.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: Thank you very much, Madam Chair.

chapters.summary: Chair Louis introduces LU 67, 68, 69, and 71 for the Monitor Point project in Council Member Restler's district, describing the mixed-use development with a museum, market-rate building, and affordable housing building on MTA-controlled land.
chapters.title: Chair Louis introduces Monitor Point rezoning proposal in Greenpoint

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Thank you, Councilmember Brewer.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The other vote concerns LU 67, 68, 69, and 71, related to the Monitor Point rezoning proposal in Councilmember Ressler's district.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The applicant is seeking to develop a mixed-use residential project in Greenpoint, Brooklyn.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: That would consist of a museum building, a market rate building with MIH permanently affordable housing known as the West Building, and a 100% affordable housing building known as the East Building.

chapters.summary: Louis explains the site's uniqueness: publicly owned MTA land in an area rezoned before MIH requirements, offering a rare chance to build affordable housing for low-income New Yorkers alongside waterfront green space.
chapters.title: Chair Louis on unique opportunity for affordable housing on publicly owned Greenpoint waterfront

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Let me first address the affordable housing component of this project.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This is a unique project for two reasons.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The majority of the development site consists of a publicly owned land controlled by the MTA.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Although there has been a lot of housing development along Greenpoint Waterfront in the past decade, the area was rezoned prior to the city's mandatory inclusionary housing requirements.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: As a result, the affordable housing built to date has been at income levels that are not affordable to low-income New Yorkers.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This large, publicly owned site is a unique opportunity to build hundreds of units of affordable housing that low-income New Yorkers can actually afford in an area that has an overwhelmingly...

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: amount of market rate housing.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This project is also unique because it provides the opportunity to pair affordable housing with green space.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Since I have been chair of the subcommittee, you have heard me state the importance of providing the needed infrastructure and services for the new housing units we approve.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The proposed project will have over one acre of open space and connect to a larger waterfront open space plan in Greenpoint and Williamsburg,

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: providing a unique opportunity to provide affordable housing with direct access to waterfront green space.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Where affordable housing can be paired with the community improvements, we should maximize the amount of affordable housings built.

chapters.summary: Louis announces modifications increasing affordable units from 460 to 662, including converting commercial space to residential, reallocating museum space, extending the development site, and adding height to the East Building.
chapters.title: Chair Louis on increased affordable units and project modifications

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: As originally proposed by the applicant, the project would have a total of 1,150 apartments, of which 460, 40%, were proposed to be permanently affordable.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Based on the modifications to the project that we are recommending, which I will describe in more detail,

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: and the leadership of the council, the project will now have an additional 202 permanently affordable apartments for a total of 662 affordable units.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: In addition, there will be over one acre of open space on the site, and the site will include a museum, an educational space to commemorate the USS Monitor, a warship launched from Greenpoint that played a pivotal role in the war.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: the Civil War.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This is truly a one-of-a-kind project.

chapters.summary: Louis describes the zoning map amendment, text amendment for MIH, special permit for large-scale development with bulk modifications, and city map change to remove park designation, noting LU 70 was withdrawn.
chapters.title: Chair Louis details four remaining land use actions for Monitor Point

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The proposal initially involved five land use actions.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: However, the applicant has withdrawn its application regarding LU-70 for a special permit modifying the applicable loading berth

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: requirement and it is no longer needed and we will vote to file that action.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: There are four remaining actions.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: First, a zoning map amendment to change the existing manufacturing M3-1 zoning district to a residential R6 and R8 district with a C2-4 commercial overlay.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Second, a zoning text amendment to map a mandatory inclusionary housing area,

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: modify the density, bulk, and park designation regulations for a waterfront blocks located in the Greenpoint-Williamsburg Waterfront Access Plan, and certain waivers available for a large-scale general development.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Third, a special permit to create a large-scale general development and modify bulk regulations that would otherwise apply to the proposed development, including

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: a reduction in the required width for a portion of the waterfront publicly accessible area along the project shoreline.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: The fourth action is a change to the city's map to remove the previous park's designation over the western portion of the development site, which is privately owned.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: owned by the parties that will oversee the monitor museum.

chapters.summary: Louis explains changes to the special permit drawings to increase affordable apartments, modifications to the waterfront publicly accessible area waiver, and notes Council Member Restler's support.
chapters.title: Chair Louis on modifications to maximize affordable housing and open space

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: In order to maximize both the affordable housing on this publicly owned site and the amount of open space, we are recommending the following modifications.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Changes to the special permit drawing for the large-scale general development, which will allow the developer to increase the number of affordable apartments provided.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This is achieved in several ways.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: By converting previously proposed commercial space to residential space, by reallocating some of the space previously allocated to the museum to more housing,

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: by extending the development site to include all the MTA-controlled land, and by providing additional height to the East Building.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Stand at ease.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: We are also modifying the waiver granted to decrease the width of a portion of the required waterfront publicly accessible area, which will maximize the amount of public open space on the site.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Councilmember Ressler supports this application as modified, and I recognize Councilmember Ressler for remarks.

chapters.summary: Restler recounts the five-year negotiation, from initial opposition to reaching a final agreement, with affordable housing increased from 25% to 50% of units, totaling 662 affordable apartments.
chapters.title: Council Member Restler on Monitor Point — five-year negotiation history and affordable housing increase

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you so much, Chair Lewis.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I just want to apologize to every member of the committee for my tardiness.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I'm sorry to keep each of you waiting.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I really apologize.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We just moments ago were able to reach a final agreement on this project.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: About five years ago, it was July or August of the summer of 2021, shortly after I won my primary, and I remember screaming on the phone to Will Schwartz at the MTA about how I thought this project was a total non-starter.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: It's come an extraordinarily long way, and I am pleased today to express my support for the amended proposal for a majority affordable housing development known as Monitor Point at 40 Quay Street in Greenpoint.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I've been clear over these five years that any development of this publicly owned land must be primarily in the public interest.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I'm grateful to the hundreds, actually thousands of neighbors who've reached out to me and my office to share their perspective.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: In direct response to our feedback, the applicant, Gotham, has amended their proposal to increase the share of permanently affordable housing from about 25% of the units to 50% of the units, nearly tripling the number of actual affordable housing units that are going to be built in our community.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: At my insistence, this development will include 662 affordable apartments in this 13 plus 100 unit development.

chapters.summary: Restler specifies that 50% of affordable units are deeply affordable, 161 units for seniors, and 110 units of supportive housing for formerly homeless individuals with on-site services.
chapters.title: Restler details affordability levels — deeply affordable, senior, and supportive housing units

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 50% of those affordable units are going to be deeply affordable.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So to give you a sense, a family of three making $76,300 a year will be able to rent a two-bedroom apartment at $1,822 a month.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That is deep affordability.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 26% of the apartments will be for moderate-income households, 172 units.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 161 of the affordable homes will be designated for seniors, and we all know the need for affordable housing for seniors.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That means that a senior making $34,600 a year can rent a one-bedroom apartment for just $911.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And 30% of the units, and this was very important to me personally, 110 apartments will be supportive housing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That's deeply affordable housing for formerly homeless individuals with on-site services to ensure that we are actually meeting the moment of our homelessness crisis.

chapters.summary: Restler describes expanded public green space, a 40-foot waterfront esplanade, $300,000 annual contribution to Bushwick Inlet Park, MTA relocation from Box Street Park, and flood protection measures.
chapters.title: Restler on green space, Bushwick Inlet Park, and resiliency investments

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: This development will also yield significant green space benefits for our community and strengthen resiliency.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: This project is adjacent to Bushwick Inlet Park, a long-promised...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 27-acre park that was central promise of the 2005 rezoning.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The vast majority of the park remains unremediated and fenced off.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The mayor visited the future park site with me and told me that he is committed to fulfilling the promise of this park and completing Bushwick Inlet Park.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The developer will be contributing $300,000 annually for the extent of this agreement, which I believe is 99 years, to maintaining and operating Bushwick Inlet Park.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And dedicated funds will go to the North Brooklyn Parks Alliance, a local conservancy group, to help maintain that space.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The project will include more public green space than originally proposed.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The waterfront esplanade will now be 40 feet wide, resulting in a total addition of 52,000 square feet of publicly accessible waterfront space linking Greenpoint directly to the Motiva parcel of Bushwick Inlet Park.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There will also be two new public bathrooms in the East Building for park users and neighbors who just got to go.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And as was promised 20 years ago, the MTA Emergency Response Unit will be relocated, finally, from the promised Box Street Park in the northern tip of Greenpoint to East Williamsburg.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The MTA is going to be responsible for demolishing their building at Box Street Park and paving over that land to make way for the final section of that promised park.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The development will help protect against flooding by building a new bulkhead along the waterfront edge and by building stormwater retention systems to decrease the load on our local sewer systems.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The buildings and the green space will also be built in an elevation to help mitigate inland flooding.

chapters.summary: Restler highlights a $60 million elevator project at Nassau Avenue G station, 2,700 square feet of community facility space for local nonprofits, and union construction and building service jobs.
chapters.title: Restler on infrastructure investments — Nassau Avenue G station accessibility and community facility space

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we have secured...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: consequential investments to ensure our infrastructure can meet this moment and the growing needs of our community.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There will be a new, the Nassau Avenue G station will now be fully accessible with a new elevator going in.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: That is an approximately $60 million project and it will be executed in the current MTA capital plan.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There will also be 2,700 square feet of community facility space for local nonprofits in the Monitor Point development.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Look, most importantly, this project will add good, well, let me add, this project will add good union jobs, work partnering with the laborers on construction and partnering with 32BJ.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Once these buildings are built, I want to thank both of those unions for their support of this project.

chapters.summary: Restler thanks Speaker Menon, Chair Lewis, Land Use Chair Riley, and the Council Land Use Team for their support through the difficult negotiation.
chapters.title: Restler on community impact and acknowledgments — Speaker, chairs, and land use team

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I also want to thank the Monitor Museum for partnering with the applicant to make this happen.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And this project will yield a new 18,000 square foot museum.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I want to thank George and Janet for helping in that regard as well.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But look, most importantly to me, in a neighborhood where we have seen rents skyrocket, where we've lost 15,000 Latino residents in the last

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: 15 years, where we've seen rents increase by 60% in just the last six years, this development will finally add desperately needed affordable housing that our community can actually afford.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And it will help some of our most vulnerable neighbors access housing as our city suffers from a historic affordability crisis.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I know that many of my neighbors will be disappointed in this outcome.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: But I believe this is ultimately a positive project for our community, and that's why I'm supporting it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: This was far and away the hardest negotiation I've had to be a part of in my five years in the council, and I just want to say a handful of thank yous.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: First and foremost to Speaker Menon.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: She had my back every single step of the way on this project.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We discussed what I could support.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: She said she agreed with me and then supported me, and her and her team were phenomenal.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: We would not have gotten to this outcome without her help.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I really want to thank the chair of this committee, Chair Lewis, for leading a great hearing and for her support, and especially want to thank the land use chair, Kevin Riley, for coming out with me to tour Monitor Point, to see the site, to understand why this was so important to me, and for helping us get to the right place.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I know his commitment to affordable housing is deep, and I know he wanted us to get here, and I'm thankful for his help in getting us there.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: The Council Land Use Team was superb.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Ann, Lena, Omar were all deeply involved and did a terrific job, and I especially want to thank Brian Paul and William Vidal, who just offered exceptional advice and counsel at all hours of the day and night over a very long period of time to help us get here.

chapters.summary: Restler expresses gratitude to the MTA, Gotham Organization developers, the Mumdani administration, and community advocates including Save the Inlet and Churches United for Fair Housing.
chapters.title: Restler thanks MTA, Gotham Organization, and administration partners

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I really am grateful to all the members of the Council Land Use Team, so thank you for your hard work on this.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I want to thank the MTA that really stepped up at the end of this project to help make this happen.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Sean Fitzpatrick is an old friend and a really talented guy, and we were lucky to have his involvement here.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I was really tough on the guys at Gotham, and they put up with it, and they said to me all along, Lincoln, we like building affordable.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: housing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: If we can make the financing work, we'll be happy to do it.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And they stuck true to their word, and we together pushed each in our own ways to get the affordable investment we needed.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I really want to thank Brian Kelly in particular for sticking with it and pushing and finding a path forward.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: David Pickett for his leadership, Varun, Simeon, and the whole Gotham team for being good partners.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And we're looking forward to working with them in the years to come.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I want to thank, as I did, the Greenpoint Modern Museum folks, George and Janet.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I really want to thank the Mumdani administration.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I think this was probably their first.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: big, hairy, hard ULURP of their first six months.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And Deputy Mayor Bozorg, Genevieve, Arvind, Patrick Love, Jelani, Deputy Mayor Kersen, and others all engaged with an open mind.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And even when they told me that what I was asking for was really, really difficult or maybe impossible, they kept trying to figure out a way to make it work.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: There are many community members who pushed hard here.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Some for this project, some against, and I'm grateful to all of them.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I want to start by just thanking the Save the Inlet folks and the friends of Bushwick Inlet Park, Catherine Thompson, Steve Chesler, Trina McKeever, and many others.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Nobody's been more committed to the park over the last 20 years than them, and I'm hopeful that this...

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm hopeful that ultimately this is going to be something that they can see adds positive benefit for our community.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I also really want to thank Rob Solano and Churches United for Fair Housing.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: who really fought hard with me for more affordable housing on this site.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And last but not least, I don't mean to brag, but I think I have the best team in the city council.

chapters.summary: Restler praises his district staff — Hannah, Eliza, and Molly — for their exceptional work on the Monitor Point project.
chapters.title: Restler thanks his staff team

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And they are just exceptionally smart and hardworking and fun and give all the best advice.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I'm really, really lucky.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So Hannah leads our land use work and is our district director and is remarkable.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Eliza leads all of our works on communications and helped us really hone our message here.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And Molly's my chief of staff.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: They're all really great, and I'm very lucky to get to work with them.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: So with that, I just want to say thank you to everybody for your patience and support and for all the colleagues who had my back through a difficult process.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: And I'm pleased with the outcome that we were able to achieve.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: Thank you very much.

chapters.summary: Louis asks for questions or remarks, receives none, and calls for a roll call vote on all LU items and a motion to file LU 70.
chapters.title: Chair Louis calls for vote on all items

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Council, are there any members of, are there any council members with questions or remarks at this time?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Members, sorry.

utterances.speaker: Gale A. Brewer
utterances.text: No, Chair.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Okay.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I now call for a vote.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: To approve modifications LU 67, 68, 69, and 71 related to the monitor point rezoning proposal, LU 72 through 74 related to the Do It Clinton Park North rezoning proposal at 801 11th Avenue,

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: and LU 75 through 77 related to the Do It Clinton Park North rezoning proposal at 629 West 54th Street.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: And finally, pursuant to Council Rule 11.60b, I make a motion to file LU 70 to remove the item from the Council's calendar.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Council, please call the roll.

chapters.summary: All nine subcommittee members vote aye on all items, with several offering congratulations to Council Members Brewer and Restler.
chapters.title: Roll call vote on all rezoning items and filing of LU 70

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Chair Lewis?

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: Aye on all and congratulations.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Council Member Fares?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: I vote aye and all.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Council Member Shulman?

utterances.speaker: Amanda C. Farías
utterances.text: Aye and all, and congratulations to everyone.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Council Member Salam?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: A vote I own all.

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: Congratulations.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Councilmember Felder?

utterances.speaker: Yusef Salaam
utterances.text: I know.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Councilmember Encarnacion.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: I vote aye, no.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Councilmember Sanchez.

utterances.speaker: Lincoln Restler
utterances.text: I vote aye or no.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Councilmember Thomas Henry?

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Aye on all.

utterances.speaker: Shanel Thomas-Henry
utterances.text: Congrats.

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: Council Member Carr.

utterances.speaker: David M. Carr
utterances.text: With congratulations to Council Members Brewer and Ressler, I vote aye on all.

chapters.summary: Council staff announces the vote of nine affirmative, zero opposing, zero abstentions; all items are approved and referred to the full Land Use Committee. Chair Louis adjourns the meeting.
chapters.title: Vote outcome: all items approved 9-0 and referred to full Land Use Committee

utterances.speaker: Council Staff
utterances.text: By a vote of nine in the affirmative, zero opposing, and zero abstention, the items are approved as described by the chair, and these items are now referred to the full land use committee.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: That concludes today's business.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: I would like to thank members of the public, my colleagues, subcommittee, council, land use, and council staff, and the sergeant at arms for participating in today's meeting.

utterances.speaker: Farah N. Louis
utterances.text: This meeting is hereby adjourned.

summary: Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises votes on two major rezoning proposals: Dewitt Clinton Park North in Manhattan and Monitor Point in Greenpoint, Brooklyn.
Council Member Brewer supports the Dewitt Clinton Park North project but criticizes the limited affordable housing concessions and calls for more workforce housing.
Council Member Restler champions the Monitor Point development, which increases affordable units to 662 (50% of the project) and includes supportive housing, senior units, and park investments.
title: Subcommittee on Zoning and Franchises
